---
description: "A note on process. This is live, in-progress work written in the open, with AI in the loop and the judgment mine."
toc: false
---

# How This Book Is Being Written {.unnumbered}

A note on process, since it matters here. This book is written in the open, and
it is a live experiment as much as a text. It is easy to have opinions about AI
from the sidelines. I think it is harder, and more useful, to work with these
systems every day and feel where they help and where they fail. If I am going to
argue that engineering is changing because of AI, I should experience that change
firsthand rather than theorize about it from a distance.

The ideas, arguments, structure, and direction here are mine. I spend most of my
time thinking through the questions I want to answer, shaping the narrative, and
deciding what the contribution is. I then use a workflow of several AI agents to
brainstorm alternatives, challenge my assumptions, reorganize material, critique
drafts, and refine the writing. What I am still learning is how to govern that
process, knowing when to accept a suggestion, when to reject it outright, and when
fluent language quietly hides a weak idea. That judgment is mine to develop, not
something I hand to the AI, and I will not develop it by avoiding these tools.

The work does not move in a straight line. It goes two steps forward and one step
back. When things seem to be going well, I get confident and let the engine run,
and often that is exactly when it inverts and I have to walk it back. This is also
new ground. I want to build this area of work with my research group, and there is
no finished resource to go read. So I am writing it in pieces, assembling parts,
and trying to sift a coherent picture out of them.

Some of this will be rough, and much of it will change. I have always believed in
building in the open, and I do not see publishing as the end of the process. I see
it as the start of a conversation. If a passage is vague, imprecise, or wrong, or
if you just want to talk it through, please bear with me and feel free to
[reach out](https://vijay.seas.harvard.edu).

Vijay Janapa Reddi
